# Bitfield Docs

> World's fastest plug-in runtime and database for founders who can't afford to slow down when plans change.

These pages are public customer documentation. They describe the public product, public Runtime Kit surface, active-device language, and benchmark claim categories.

## Pages

- [Bitfield Docs](index): The public entry point and path chooser for understanding Bitfield, trying it, building a first screen, checking proof, or managing account access.
- [Quickstart](start/quickstart): A first-result tutorial for account access, device activation, Runtime Kit imports, reading prepared data, sending a target request, and handling first failures.
- [Get your key](start/get-your-key): Explains the account portal path for account creation, trial or plan access, email confirmation, key access, first device activation, recovery, and next steps.
- [What is Bitfield?](concepts/what-is-bitfield): Explains Bitfield without assuming the reader already knows database or runtime vocabulary.
- [Database and runtime](concepts/database-and-runtime): Defines the public database and runtime model.
- [Storage shape](concepts/storage-shape): Explains local durable truth, memory-mapped local files, content identity, and the current view.
- [Content-addressed storage](concepts/content-addressed-storage): Defines content addresses and states what the public docs do not expose.
- [Slots](concepts/slots): Explains slots as named runnable doors that features can call without importing each other.
- [Active devices](concepts/active-devices): Defines active devices for pricing, keys, and account management.
- [JavaScript Runtime Kit](runtime-kit/javascript): Maps the public Runtime Kit flow from package boundary to prepared reads and named target requests.
- [Package to screen](runtime-kit/package-to-screen): Walks from a package boundary through admitted material, prepared reads, target requests, and React rendering.
- [Runtime Kit concept map](runtime-kit/concept-map): Explains the full Runtime Kit concept chain with product scenes, visual flow, anti-patterns, and links to tutorial, recipe, and reference pages.
- [Placeable surfaces](runtime-kit/placeable-surfaces): Explains the placeable-surface composition primitive with visual maps: package material becomes prepared reads and targets, descriptors declare regions and allowed names, and a dumb shell/sidebar places surfaces without product-specific branches.
- [Read data in React](runtime-kit/use-bitfield-data): Shows selectors, render states, request pairing, and what React components should not own.
- [Send a request](runtime-kit/send-request): Shows JSON, text, byte payloads, reply decoding, cancellation, and target-boundary mistakes.
- [Packages](runtime-kit/packages): Explains package sets, records, stored bytes, callable slots, validation rules, and safe package examples.
- [Local state](runtime-kit/local-state): Explains stored data, Runtime Kit package state, activation material, and what users should not hand-edit.
- [Build with AI agents](runtime-kit/ai-build-rules): Gives AI-agent prompt packs, bad-output corrections, review maps, and public Runtime Kit boundaries for generated feature work.
- [Troubleshooting](runtime-kit/troubleshooting): Maps Runtime Kit symptoms to the public chain link that failed and gives safe repair checks.
- [Runtime Kit Cookbook](runtime-kit/cookbook/index): Indexes Runtime Kit cookbook entries for packages, package-owned bytes, callable slots, React surfaces, and placeable surface product loops.
- [Package set with one record](runtime-kit/cookbook/package-set-with-one-record): Shows the smallest package boundary with one durable package record.
- [Package-owned file](runtime-kit/cookbook/package-owned-file): Shows how a package declares package-owned bytes without reaching outside its folder.
- [Callable package slot](runtime-kit/cookbook/callable-package-slot): Shows how a package declares a callable slot and how app code sends a request to it.
- [React surface for package data](runtime-kit/cookbook/react-surface-for-package-data): Shows the app-facing read and request shape without exposing package implementation details to React.
- [Placeable surface product loop](runtime-kit/cookbook/placeable-surface-product-loop): Shows the source-owned cookbook succession for placeable surfaces: package boundary, descriptors, generic shell, surface bodies, and review checklist.
- [Build with Bitfield](build-your-own-surface/index): Maps the full Bitfield build loop from product moment to package, surface, target, shell, device, and account access.
- [App surfaces](build-your-own-surface/app-surfaces): Shows how product screens read prepared data, render every state, and send named target requests.
- [Package authoring](build-your-own-surface/package-authoring): Explains how to draw a feature boundary with records, package-owned files, callable slots, expected results, and failures.
- [Target design](build-your-own-surface/target-design): Gives naming, payload, reply, and failure rules for callable targets that surfaces can request safely.
- [Product shells](build-your-own-surface/product-shells): Defines the shell boundary for navigation, layout, account/device state, and surface placement.
- [Device and deployment shape](build-your-own-surface/device-deployment): Connects product packages and surfaces to account keys, activated devices, local state, and rollout decisions.
- [Build your own surface](build-your-own-surface/sdk-boundary): Defines the public SDK boundary pattern: simple app functions outside, Bitfield setup behind the plug.
- [Account and key flow](activation/account-and-key-flow): Explains the account portal path for creating an account, choosing access, paying securely, getting key access, and activating a device.
- [Trials and billing](activation/trials-and-billing): Explains trial billing and active-device ranges without turning pricing into hidden math.
- [Active devices](activation/active-devices): Defines active devices, local activation state, account-owned authorization, replacement, revocation, and customer-safe boundaries.
- [Plan changes and cancellation](activation/plan-changes-and-cancellation): Explains plan management, cancellation, invoice/refund routing, account deletion, recovery, and support links.
- [How Bitfield is this fast](proof/how-bitfield-is-fast): Explains warm local reads, memory mapping, content addresses, and non-claims.
- [Warm and cold paths](proof/warm-and-cold-paths): Defines warm local read, cold local read, content address lookup, durable batch write, runtime call, and network command.
- [Benchmark ledger](proof/benchmark-ledger): Lists public speed claims with the work category attached.
- [Measurement methodology](proof/methodology): Explains warm reads, cold reads, hash lookup, durable writes, and why each number needs its category.
- [Comparison guardrails](proof/comparison-guardrails): Explains which proof rows can be compared, which comparisons are category mistakes, and how to keep benchmark work shapes honest.
- [Proof objections](proof/objection-faq): Answers skeptical questions about cold reads, network latency, batch ceilings, content addressing, and public disclosure boundaries.
- [Runtime Kit API](reference/runtime-kit-api): Reference for sendRequestToBitfieldTarget, useBitfieldData, payload conversion, cancellation, selectors, return states, valid examples, invalid examples, and public boundaries.
- [Claim ledger](reference/claim-ledger): Documents the public claim-ledger fields that keep speed claims honest.
- [Package boundary](reference/package-boundary): Lists package boundary fields, record/stored-bytes/slot thing types, payload choices, method rules, path safety, valid examples, invalid examples, and common failures.
- [Changelog](changelog/index): The public change log for docs and customer-visible product behavior.

## Public reading rules

- Keep every speed number attached to its measured category.
- Keep non-claims attached to benchmark rows.
- Treat warm local reads, cold local reads, content address lookups, durable batch writes, runtime calls, and network commands as different jobs.
- Do not infer private implementation details from public mechanism categories.

## Public API surface

- `@bitfield/runtime-kit`: `sendRequestToBitfieldTarget(...)`
- `@bitfield/runtime-kit/react`: `useBitfieldData(...)`

## Benchmark boundary

- 0.68ns warm local read: warm-local-read. The data is local and already warm, so Bitfield can read known local bytes without sending a request to a separate database process. Not claiming: A cold disk read takes less than a nanosecond. A network round trip disappears. Every database benchmark measures the same job.
- 91.7ns hash lookup: content-address-lookup. Bitfield first resolves the content address, which means it uses the data fingerprint to find the bytes, then reads them. Not claiming: This is the same category as the H0 exact read. This includes networked application latency.
- 316ns durable batch write comparison: durable-batch-write. The measured work includes the saved-write contract for a batch, then divides the batch cost across the written items. Not claiming: Every single isolated write has 316ns wall-clock latency. This is the same category as a networked managed database write.
- 0.59ns batched engine write ceiling: batched-write-ceiling. This is the optimized write path divided across a full batch. It is a ceiling number, not a replacement for durable write comparison rows. Not claiming: A full saved write is always 0.59ns. This number should be compared directly to every vendor write benchmark.

Cold storage, network trips, and managed database requests are different categories from warm local reads.
